title: Chapter 7: Oat Milk and First Contact
content: The dean's office had become the unofficial headquarters of the Blackwood Sanctuary.

By sunrise on day two, Roxanne had acquired: the campus (owned), one formerly homicidal chainsaw entity (currently stationed at the front gate), one freshly unsealed Primordial god (currently staring at the espresso machine with intense confusion), and approximately forty-seven survivors who had emerged from various hiding spots once they realized the campus was safe.

Lena had organized them into groups: able-bodied, injured, children, and everyone else. She'd done this while Roxanne was in the Cathedral, and she'd done it with the same quiet efficiency she applied to organizing her charcoal grades by hardness.

"Forty-seven people," Lena reported, flipping through her sketchbook where she'd taken notes. "Twelve children under fourteen. Eight injured — mostly cuts and panic, one broken ankle. Twenty-seven able adults, including three nursing students, one actual nurse, and a guy who claims he was a barista."
"A barista?" Roxanne perked up.
"That's what you focused on?"
"That's the most useful skill anyone has mentioned. The nurse is obviously valuable. But a barista means I can stop making my own coffee."
Silas, who had been examining the espresso machine with the careful attention of someone studying an alien artifact, finally spoke.
"I believe I understand this device now. The water goes here. The ground beans go here. Pressure forces the water through —"
"Silas. You were sealed for four billion years. You don't need to make coffee."
"I want to make coffee. I've never made coffee. I've made stars, black holes, and at least three galaxies. But never coffee."
Roxanne looked at Lena.
"Is this real?"
"He's a god. They're dramatic about everything."
By midday, the Sanctuary had a routine.

Roxanne's new barista — a guy named Derek with sleeve tattoos and an expression that said he'd seen things — set up a coffee station in the Fine Arts lobby. The nursing students converted the campus health center into a triage unit. Someone found a working projector and set up a movie screening for the kids in the lecture hall.
It wasn't normal. But it was something close enough to normal that people stopped crying.
Roxanne stood on the quad, watching her small civilization take shape.
Silas stood beside her. His shadow-coat moved independently of the wind — a constant reminder that he wasn't human, no matter how human he looked.
"How long do we have?" she asked.
"Before what?"
"Before the other Primordials wake up. Before they come looking for you."
Silas was quiet for a moment.

"The card's activation was a signal. Every major power in the Nether-Grid felt it. The ones who remember the Primordial Bloodline will be... curious. The ones who remember me will be alarmed. And the ones who sealed me will be angry."
"How long?"

"Days. Maybe a week. The Nether-Grid doesn't move quickly — it's a bureaucracy, not an army. There will be inquiries. Meetings. Votes. By the time they decide to act, we'll have maybe... seventy-two hours of warning."
"Seventy-two hours. That's not much."
"It's more than most civilizations get."
Roxanne turned to face him.
"Can you fight them? All of them?"
"Not at full strength. The seal drained me. I need time to recover — and currency."
"Currency?"
"Gods don't run on prayers anymore. They run on Obols. The Nether-Grid economy replaced the old systems. If I want my power back, I need to buy it."
Roxanne pulled out her card.
"How much?"
Silas looked at her. There was something new in his expression — surprise, maybe. Or gratitude.
"You would spend your currency on me?"
"You're my employee. I invest in my employees."
"I'm a god."
"Same thing. How much?"
Silas smiled. It was a dangerous smile.
"To restore me to full Primordial strength? Approximately two billion Obols."
Roxanne's eye twitched.
"Two billion."

"The seal did significant damage. My divine architecture needs reconstruction. Power conduits, manifestation matrices, existential anchor points — the terminology doesn't translate well, but the price is accurate."
"I have infinity. But two billion is still... a lot."
"Break it into phases. One hundred million now — restore my core functions. Another five hundred million over the next week for combat capability. The rest can wait."
Roxanne considered this.
"You've thought about this."
"I've had four billion years to plan my recovery. The fact that the heir showed up with an infinite card was not in any of my projections."
"So I'm a variable."
"You're a very pleasant variable."
She laughed despite herself.
"Okay. One hundred million for core functions. Let's see what you can do."
She held up the card.
*PURCHASE: SILAS — CORE FUNCTION RESTORATION. PRICE: 100,000,000 OBOLS.*
*APPROVED.*

Silas's eyes flared. Not red — gold. The same gold as the symbols that had bound him. Light poured from his form, resolving into a shape that Roxanne's brain couldn't fully process — wings made of shadow, eyes that blinked in places eyes shouldn't be, a presence that pressed against reality like a hand against a window.
Then it settled.
He was human-shaped again. But the shadows around him were deeper. The air around him was colder. And when he smiled, there was something in it that hadn't been there before.
"Thank you," he said. And he meant it.

*End of Chapter 7*
\n---\n
By midday, the Sanctuary had a routine.

Roxanne's new barista — a guy named Derek with full-sleeve tattoos and the thousand-yard stare of someone who had survived the first three days of the apocalypse — set up a coffee station in the Fine Arts lobby. The nursing students commandeered the campus health center. Someone found a working projector and started playing animated movies for the kids in the lecture hall.
Roxanne stood on the quad, watching her small civilization take shape. Silas stood beside her, his shadow-coat rippling in a wind that didn't touch anything else.
"How long do we have?" she asked.
"Before what?"
"Before the other Primordials figure out that you're free."

Silas considered. "The card's activation was a signal. Every major power in the Nether-Grid felt it. The ones who remember the Primordial bloodline will be curious. The ones who remember me will be alarmed. The ones who sealed me will be angry."
"How long?"
"Days. Maybe a week. The Exchange moves slowly — it's a bureaucracy, not an army. There will be inquiries. Meetings. Votes. They'll want to assess before they act."
"So we have a week."
"At most."
She looked at the campus. At the people. At the fragile peace she had built.
"Then let's make it count."
By mid-afternoon, Roxanne had a list.
Campus assets: 12 buildings, 1 underground ley node cathedral, 1 working espresso machine, 47 survivors, 1 freed god, 1 formerly-hostile executioner entity, 47 varied survival skills.
Needs: food, water purification, medical supplies, bedding, warm clothing, ammunition for the survivor militia, and a working radio to communicate with other pockets of civilization.
The card could solve most of these. But she didn't want to become dependent on it. What if it stopped working? What if someone found a way to block it? What if she dropped it down a drain?
"You're thinking about failure cases," Silas observed from across the room, where he was reading a campus map.
"I'm an art student. We're trained to think about what could go wrong."
"That's unusual. Most art students think about what could go right."
"Most art students aren't running a post-apocalyptic sanctuary."
"Fair point."
She tapped the map. "We need to secure the perimeter. The campus is too open. Anyone could walk in."
"The Executioner is at the gate. The Enforcement units are patrolling the walls. That's a significant deterrent."
"It's not enough. I want walls. Actual walls. And a check-in system for survivors."
"You want a bureaucracy."
"I want an organized sanctuary. Bureaucracy is just organization with paperwork."
"What about the card? Can it buy walls?"
She looked at the card.
*DISPLAY: FORTIFICATION OPTIONS — BLACKWOOD UNIVERSITY*

A list appeared: stone walls, energy barriers, contract-based protection wards, summoned guardian entities. Prices ranged from 50,000 Obols for basic stone walls to 8 million for a full ley-node-powered barrier.
"I can buy a force field for 8 million," she said.
"That's less than a coffee shop in Manhattan used to cost."
"I know. I'm buying it."

She tapped the card. The transaction processed. A low hum spread across the campus as the barrier activated — translucent, shimmering, visible only at the edges where it intersected with the violet sky.
"There. Now we're safe."
"Safer," Silas corrected. "Not safe."
"I'll take 'safer.'"
